coredata.dc:title: Coupling of graphene quantum dots with MnO2 nanosheets for boosting capacitive storage in ionic liquid electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Hangtian
Li, Lingyun
Shi, Minjie
Xiao, Peng
Liu, Yuting
Yan, Xingbin
coredata.dc:description: Utilizing ionic liquid (IL) as electrolyte to fabricate supercapacitor is an effective strategy for increasing its operating voltage and energy density. Although manganese dioxide (MnO2) can exhibit pseudocapacitive storage behavior in some specific IL electrolytes, it still suffers from low specific capacitance as well as unsatisfactory kinetics. Herein, a new design of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) integrated with MnO2 nanosheets is proposed to construct a GQDs@MnO2 composite electrode for IL-based supercapacitor. Synergistically coupling of GQDs with MnO2 nanosheets provides a 3D nanoflower architecture with increased surface area, enhanced electrochemical kinetics and excellent structural integrity. The GQDs play an important role in modifying the density of state and energy bandgap as well as enhance the electronic conductivity of GQDs@MnO2 electrode. These properties endow the superior capacitive storage, rapid charge–discharge response and high electrochemical reversibility for GQDs@MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte. For real-life application, a high-performance flexible IL-based supercapacitor is assembled, which can deliver a high energy density (82.2 Wh kg−1), a high power density (11.6 kW kg−1), and long-term cycle performance upon the straight and bent states, suggesting its great potential in energy-related technologies and portable electronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capacitive Storage
Manganese oxide
Graphene Quantum Dots
Ionic liquid
Flexible supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural features of GQDs@MnO2 composite
Electrochemical behaviors and capacitive storage mechanism
Fabrication and application demonstration of flexible supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis route for the GQDs@MnO2 composite. (a) SEM image, (c) TEM image, (d) corresponding elemental mappings of C, Mn and O elements, (e, f) HRTEM images and (g) XRD pattern for GQDs@MnO2 composite.

(a) Overall XPS survey spectrum of GQDs@MnO2 composite. Inset shows the magnified region of K 2p spectrum. (b) C 1 s and (c) Mn 2p high-resolution spectrum, (d) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of GQDs@MnO2 composite.

(a) Comparative CV curves of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes tested in EMIMBF4 IL electrolyte. (b) CV profiles scaning at various rates of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode. (c) Raman spectra and (d) the correspoding contour map of GQDs@MnO2 electrode electrode during different charging-discharging steps.

(a) GCD profiles and (b) Capacitive storage of GQDs@MnO2 electrode compared with metal oxide-based electrodes in IL electrolyte. (c) Rate capabilities of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrode. DFT calculations about the (d) TDOS patterns for pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2. The corresponding PDOS patterns of (e) pristine MnO2 and (f) GQDs@MnO2. (g) EIS curves, (h) bode plots, and (i) Radar chart compares the five figure-of-merits for pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes in IL electrolyte.

(a) Fabrication procedure and (b) the digital photo for the flexible supercapacitor using IL electrolyte. (c) CV curves, (d) GCD profiles and (e) Ragone plot of the flexible supercapacitor. (f) Capacitance retention of flexible device at various bending angles. (g) Long-term cycling performance at the straight and bending states.

head.ce:title: Coupling of graphene quantum dots with MnO2 nanosheets for boosting capacitive storage in ionic liquid electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Grid Jiangsu Electric Power Co., Ltd. Research Institute, Nanjing 211103, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Grid Jiangsu Electric Power Co., Ltd. Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Grid Jiangsu Electric Power Co., Ltd. Research Institute, Nanjing 211103, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing ionic liquid (IL) as electrolyte to fabricate supercapacitor is an effective strategy for increasing its operating voltage and energy density. Although manganese dioxide (MnO2) can exhibit pseudocapacitive storage behavior in some specific IL electrolytes, it still suffers from low specific capacitance as well as unsatisfactory kinetics. Herein, a new design of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) integrated with MnO2 nanosheets is proposed to construct a GQDs@MnO2 composite electrode for IL-based supercapacitor. Synergistically coupling of GQDs with MnO2 nanosheets provides a 3D nanoflower architecture with increased surface area, enhanced electrochemical kinetics and excellent structural integrity. The GQDs play an important role in modifying the density of state and energy bandgap as well as enhance the electronic conductivity of GQDs@MnO2 electrode. These properties endow the superior capacitive storage, rapid charge–discharge response and high electrochemical reversibility for GQDs@MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte. For real-life application, a high-performance flexible IL-based supercapacitor is assembled, which can deliver a high energy density (82.2 Wh kg−1), a high power density (11.6 kW kg−1), and long-term cycle performance upon the straight and bent states, suggesting its great potential in energy-related technologies and portable electronics.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to overcome the low voltage limitations of conventional acid or alkaline aqueous electrolytes, as well as the hazardous and flammable properties of organic electrolytes, ionic liquids (ILs), a kind of room-temperature molten salts [1–3], have been considered as the promising electrolytes because of their unique characteristics, including non-volatility, non-flammability, excellent thermal stability, and broad electrochemical window (>2.5 V) [3–5]. In these regards, using IL electrolytes for supercapacitors not only alleviates the environmental and safety concerns involved with organic electrolytes but also shows the enormous potential to overcome the narrow electrochemical potential windows of aqueous electrolytes [5–7]. Therefore, the blossoming demands for energy storage devices have prompted IL-based supercapacitors to be utilized and continually exploited.

Developing supercapacitors utilizing IL electrolytes, in particular electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC), has received increasing interest in energy-related applications [7–10]. Compared to the EDLCs of carbon electrodes, pseudocapacitive electrodes can undergo the redox storage process, which have much higher specific capacitance in general [11–13]. Benefiting from simple construction, easy accessibility, multiple valence states, and highly electrochemical activity, manganese dioxide (MnO2) is identified as a potential pseudocapacitive electrode material [14–17]. Chang et al. [15] firstly demonstrated the pseudocapacitive effect of MnO2 electrode in an EMI-DCA IL electrolyte, which can deliver a specific capacitance of 72F g−1. Although MnO2-based electrodes exhibit pseudocapacitive storage behaviors in some specific IL electrolytes, they still suffer from low specific capacitance with unfavorable electrochemical kinetics, which is mainly due to their low conductivity (∼10−6 S cm−1) and poor charge-transfer capability in IL electrolytes [11,12].

To address these problems, a useful attempt is to incorporate electro-active MnO2 into nanocomposites with conductive carbon materials, especially two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials. Xiao et al. [17] demonstrated the synergistic coupling between porous carbon nanosheets and MnO2 nanoneedles endows the composite electrode with enhanced electrochemical capacitance behaviors in IL electrolyte. Tuantranont et al. [18] reported a composite electrode of MnO2 nanofibers incorporated with N, S co-doped reduced graphene oxide, which is highly attractive for IL-based supercapacitors. As an emerging zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are of great interest owing to their similar characteristics to graphene and even more merits especially nanosized effect and strong quantum confinement [19,20]. When compared with common multi-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, nanosized GQDs are more flexible and versatile for constructing diverse nanostructures [21–23]. Furthermore, GQDs have impressive electrochemical conductivity, high specific surface area, as well as a large number of active edge sites, contributing to the enhancement of capacitive properties of electrode materials in supercapacitors [23–26]. Nevertheless, it is still unclear whether GQDs and electro-active MnO2 can be integrated into the composite electrode for boosting capacitive storage in IL electrolyte.

Herein, we have developed a facile approach to synthesize a 3D nanoflower-like GQDs@MnO2 composite by synergistically coupling GQDs with MnO2 nanosheets, wherein the GQDs play a crucial role in providing abundant active sites and enhancing the electronic conductivity, as well as modifying the density of state and energy bandgap of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. These features are further demonstrated by a series of electrochemical investigations and DFT calculations. As a result, the unique hybrid-structured GQDs@MnO2 composite, when for the first time utilized as a supercapacitor electrode in IL electrolyte, exhibits a considerably large specific capacitance of 208.2F g−1, outperforming previously reported metal oxide-based electrodes (most < 200F g−1) in IL electrolyte. Furtherly, we have assembled a flexible IL-based supercapacitor with reliable electrochemical behaviors and satisfactory energy density up to 82.2 Wh kg−1, further stimulating its potential application as an efficient electrochemical storage device for various energy fields.

Initially, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite through the Hummers' modified approach [27]. 0.5 g GO powder was dispersed into 40 mL N, N-dimethylformamide with an ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. Afterwards, the resulting dispersion was placed into an autoclave maintained at 200 °C for 10 h. After further dialysis and vacuum freeze-drying, the GQDs powder was obtained. Subsequently, 40 mg GQDs powder and 0.16 g KMnO4 were gradually introduced into the 80 mL deionized water. A small quantity of hydrochloric acid (∼0.5 mL) was then added into the above mixture. Although the addition of salt (KMnO4) and acid (hydrochloric acid) could change the zeta potential of the GQDs solution, the GQDs can be stably dispersed in water without precipitation and agglomeration (Fig. S1). After constant agitation at 85 ℃ for 3 h, the solid product was repeatedly centrifuged and washed with deionized water in several times, and finally vacuum-dried to achieve GQDs@MnO2 composite. In comparison, the pristine MnO2 was synthesized in the same way but without adding GQD powder.

Morphology and microstructural analysis of as-prepared samples was implemented with scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8010), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F at 200 kV), X-ray diffraction (XRD; Bruker D8 X-ray spectrometer) equipped with a 2D detector (Cu Kα, λ = 1.54 Å), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ESCLAB 250Xi), and Laser Micro-Raman Spectrometer (Raman; RENISHAW inVia). Nitrogen absorption/desorption experiments were measured with an Autosorb IQ instrument operating at 77 K, wherein the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was carried out to analyze the specific surface areas of as-prepared samples. The samples were degassed at a moderately high temperature of 150 °C for 12 h, which is owing to the good stability of functional oxygen-containing groups in GQDs under this degassed temperature (Fig. S2). DFT calculations were performed via the VASP 5.3.5 software with Perdew–Burke–Eznerhof and generalized gradient approximation.

Comprehensive electrochemical evaluations were conducted with a multichannel workstation (CS350H, Wuhan corrtest) that combines typical cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), as well as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques at the room temperature. The EIS measurements were carried out with a frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 10 mHz at an amplitude of 5 mV. Specifically, the electrode was prepared by mixing 80 wt% active materials (pristine MnO2 or GQDs@MnO2 composite), 10 wt% of acetylene black and 10 wt% polyvinylidenefluoride binder under the aid of a few drops of N-methylpyrrolidone. After being uniformly mixed, the slurry was pressed onto graphite paper as the working electrode with the mass loading (2.5 ∼ 3.0 mg cm−2), which was measured in a common 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (EMIMBF4) IL electrolyte according to the three–electrode testing approach. As a relatively cheap and readily available IL electrolyte, EMIMBF4 IL has been widely utilized as a suitable electrolyte in energy storage devices, especially supercapacitor applications (Fig. S3 and S4).

For the fabrication of flexible IL-based supercapacitor, a full-cell configuration was assembled with the as-prepared GQDs@MnO2 composite and commercial activated carbon (YP17, Kuraray chemical) as cathode and anode materials, respectively. On the basis of charge balance theory, the optimal mass ratio of activated carbon anode to GQDs@MnO2 composite cathode is 1.3:1 (See Fig. S5 and S6 in Supplementary Material for more details). Subsequently, the anode, separator and cathode were carefully packed in a sandwich arrangement (anode–separator–cathode) and encapsulated into the aluminum plastic film for packaging. After the injected process of EMIMBF4 IL electrolyte, the vacuum sealing of soft-packing flexible IL-based supercapacitor was completed by using sealing equipment. The specific capacitance of IL-based supercapacitor was calculated from the GCD curves according to the following equation: C = I
Â ·
Δt/m
Â ·
ΔV, where I is the constant discharge current, Δt is the discharging time, m is the total mass of GQDs@MnO2 cathode and activated carbon anode, and ΔV is the voltage variation during the discharge process after “IR drop”. The detailed calculation methods about the energy/power density of assembled device are supplied in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural features of GQDs@MnO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis route of the GQDs@MnO2 composite is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. In the first step, GQDs are effectively synthesized from GO via a facile solvothermal approach. As presented in Fig. S7, the as-prepared GQDs have an average size of around 3 ∼ 6 nm. In the second step, the GQDs@MnO2 composite could be successfully obtained through KMnO4 as the manganese source and GQDs as the precursor carbon source. SEM and TEM characterizations have been conducted to reveal the detailed structural information of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. In Fig. 1b and 1c, the GQDs@MnO2 composite consists of many monodispersed and uniform nanoflowers with the size of 100 nm, wherein large numbers of GQDs are anchored on MnO2 nanosheets in the composite (Fig. 1e). The integration of GQDs on the surface of MnO2 can not only provide abundant active sites for the ion/charge transport, but also enhance the electronic conductivity of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. The energy dispersive spectroscopy in Fig. 1d manifests the coexistence of C, Mn and O atoms in the GQDs@MnO2 composite, further suggesting the uniform distribution of GQDs in the MnO2 nanosheets to form a flower-like hybrid structure. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of GQDs@MnO2 composite is presented in Fig. 1f. It is evident that the measured lattice fringe spacing is about 0.34 nm, corresponding to the interlayer space of the GQDs [28,29], while the crystal lattice with the spacing of 0.24 nm is attributed to the (111) crystallographic plane of δ-phase MnO2 [29,30]. This result can also be evidenced by the XRD pattern (Fig. 1g), in which a broad peak at 24.6° indexed to the GQDs and the typical characteristic peaks at 12°, 38° and 67° assigned to the layered δ-MnO2 (JCPDS no. 80–1098 [30,31]) in the GQDs@MnO2 composite.

XPS measurement was conducted to identify the valence state and elemental arrangement of the as–synthesized GQDs@MnO2 composite. The overall survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) proves that the presence of carbon (36.5 at%), oxygen (45.7 at%), manganese (17.0 at%) and potassium (0.8 at%) elements in the GQDs@MnO2 composite. The K 2p spectrum (inset in Fig. 2a) shows two peaks located at 291.6 eV and 294.5 eV, corresponding to the spin–orbit split doublet peaks for K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2, respectively [32]. The appearance of the K 2p peaks could be ascribed to the K+ incorporated into MnO2 structure to form birnessite-type δ-phase MnO2 [33,34], which is consistent with the XRD result. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows three peaks located at around 284.8, 286.6, and 288.9 eV, which are responsible for the characteristics of C–C, C–O and C = O bonds [35], respectively. High-resolution O 1 s spectrum of GQDs@MnO2 composite is shown in Fig. S8, which can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 529.3, 530.7, and 532.2 eV, corresponding to the Mn-O, C = O and C–O bonds, respectively. The proportion of Mn-O bond of MnO2 is approximately 46.7%, while the proportion of C–O and C = O bonds resulting from the oxygen-containing groups of GQDs is 23.5% and 29.8%. The GQDs with some oxygen-containing groups make a significant contribution to the surface wettability of GQDs@MnO2 composite, thereby offering more active sites for the speedy ionic diffusion. The Mn 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows two main peaks at around 653.8 and 642.2 eV with a spin energy separation of 11.6 eV, which are the typical of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 of manganese oxide, respectively [36,37]. Furthermore, as seen from the Mn 2p spectrum at the higher binding energy, there is an obvious peak of Mn 2p1/2 at about 665.9 eV, corresponds to the Mn(IV) satellite [38], indicating that the Mn element in the manganese oxide has a dominant chemical state of Mn4+. More information about the XPS spectrum of pristine MnO2 is provided in Fig. S9, wherein the average valence state of Mn element is calculated to be 3.64. Furthermore, N2 adsorption/desorption analyses (Fig. 2d) have been performed to investigate the specific surface areas of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite. Compared to pristine MnO2 (∼77 m2 g−1), the GQDs@MnO2 composite possesses a higher specific surface area of 108 m2 g−1. The enlarged specific surface area of GQDs@MnO2 composite is mainly due to the GQDs effectively anchored on the MnO2 nanosheets, which could prevent the agglomeration and stacking of MnO2 nanosheets to a certain extent, thereby ensuring the GQDs@MnO2 composite with abundant electroactive sites for the electrochemical reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors and capacitive storage mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3a presents the CV curves of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes in EMIMBF4 IL electrolyte. By contrast, the GQDs@MnO2 electrode shows larger voltammetric current and integrated CV area than pristine MnO2 electrode, confirming its sufficient capacitive storage in IL electrolyte. Fig. 3 b gives the rate-dependent CV profiles of GQDs@MnO2 electrode under various scanning rates, wherein the curve shapes can be maintained even under high sweeping rates, indicating the rapid charge–discharge response of GQDs@MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte. Besides, some reversible peaks can be detected in the CV curves of GQDs@MnO2 electrode. This pseudocapacitance is attributed to the Faradaic redox reactions of oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs in the GQDs@MnO2 electrode [39,40]. In addition to improving the surface wettability of the electrode, oxygen-containing groups play an important role in contributing some surface pseudocapacitance by faradic redox reactions in the IL electrolyte. For a better understanding of the pseudocapacitance contribution to the total capacitance of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode, the current response (i) at a fixed potential (V) can be expressed as the combination of two separate mechanisms, namely, surface pseudocapacitive effects and diffusion-controlled insertion, as described below:
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
where the k1v and k2v1/2 correspond to the current contributions arising from the surface pseudocapacitive effects and the diffusion-controlled insertion processes, respectively. By calculating, the contribution ratios of surface pseudocapacitive effects at various scan rates can be quantitatively determined (Fig. S10), indicating that the oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs can provide some surface pseudocapacitance for GQDs@MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte.

For more insight into the pseudocapacitive reaction process of MnO2 in the GQDs@MnO2 electrode, the Raman investigation at different charge/discharge states has been carried out (Fig. 3c). On the spectra before testing, three vibrating bands are located at 506 cm−1 (v1 ), 585 cm−1 (v2 ), and 685 cm−1 (v3 ), all of which are the characteristic peaks of δ-phase MnO2 in the GQDs@MnO2 electrode [41]. One of these vibrating bands is the v1 band, which is resulting from the weak vibration of Mn–O bonds between two adjacent [MnO6] sheets. The other two vibrating bands are the v 2 and v 3 bands, which are involved with the Mn–O vibration in the basal plane of [MnO6] sheets and the Mn–O symmetric vibration in the [MnO6] octahedral, respectively [42]. Fig. 3d illustrates the corresponding 2D contour plot at various potentials. During the charging process, the intensity of v 3 band obviously increases owing to the expansion of distance between two successive layers of δ-MnO2 [41,43]. Meanwhile, the enhanced v 2 band reveals the oxidation reaction of Mn and the shortening of Mn-O in [MnO6] octahedra [32,44]. On the contrary, the intensity of v 2 and v 3 bands in discharging process shows an opposed change that is the elongation of Mn-O band associated with the reduction of Mn [44]. This reversible electrochemical reaction can also be demonstrated by the XPS analysis (Fig. S11) of GQDs@MnO2 electrode during the electrochemical process. The Mn 2p3/2 peak moves towards its lower binding energy upon discharge, suggesting the reduction of Mn(IV) to the low-valence Mn(III) state [45]. Conversely, the valence state of Mn(IV) can recover during the charging process, further indicating its high electrochemical reversibility in IL electrolyte.

Fig. 4a reveals the representative GCD profiles under various current densities of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode. All curves without obvious charging-discharging platform further suggest its pseudocapacitive behaviors in IL electrolyte. This is different from the battery-type electrodes with charging-discharging plateaus in the GCD curves owing to the relatively slow redox reaction. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S12, the GQDs@MnO2 electrode shows smaller “IR drop” than pristine MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte, testifying the enhanced electric conductivity of GQDs@MnO2 electrode that provides rapid electron transfer during the electrochemical process. Compared to pristine MnO2 electrode with low specific capacitance (only ∼ 148.4F g−1), the GQDs@MnO2 electrode exhibits superior capacitive storage behaviors with a large specific capacitance of 208.2F g−1, outperforming metal oxide-based electrodes (most being < 200F g−1, Fig. 4 b) previously reported in various IL electrolytes [17,46–51]. More information about the capacitance behaviors of the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes obtained from different GQDs addition amounts is provided in Fig. S13. Fig. 4c gives the rate capabilities of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes in IL electrolyte. As the current density increases from 1.0 to 8.0 A g−1, the rate retention can reach ∼ 69.3% for GQDs@MnO2 electrode, but only ∼ 36.2% for pristine MnO2 electrode. The higher rate capability confirms the vital role of GQDs in boosting the electron transfer of GQDs@MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte.

DFT calculations have been performed to explore the density of states and electronic properties of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite. According to the total density of state (TDOS) as shown in Fig. 4d, the pristine MnO2 possesses a distinct separation between the conduction and valence bands at the Fermi level with a bandgap of ∼ 1.59 eV. By contrast, the energy bandgap is drastically reduced to ∼ 0.33 eV in the GQDs@MnO2 composite, suggesting that more charge carriers could be introduced into the GQDs@MnO2 composite accompanied with improved electronic conductivity. According to the standard four probe-method, the conductivity of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite were measured to be 9.13 × 10−4 and 6.52 × 10−3 S·cm−1, respectively, indicating the enhanced electronic conductivity of the GQDs@MnO2 composite due to the introduction of GQDs. Different from the bottom-up approaches, the graphitic GQDs with good conductivity have been effectively achieved from GO nanosheets by using the top-down approach, wherein only a few oxygen-containing groups exist on the surface of GQDs after the hydrothermal treatment under high temperature. Therefore, the resultant GQDs are nanometer-sized graphene sheets with excellent electron mobility, which is favorable to increase the electronic conductivity and charge-transport capability of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. Fig. 4e and 4f illustrate the partial density of state (PDOS) for pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite, respectively. Stronger Mn 3d and O 2p electron states can be observed in the GQDs@MnO2 composite, indicating the enhanced Mn and O electrochemical activities and the promoted electron delocalization after MnO2 integrated with GQD [52,53], thereby ensuring the MnO2@GQD electrode with favorable electrochemical kinetics.

Fig. 4 g shows the comparative EIS plots of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes in IL electrolyte. The point intersecting the real axis involves the equivalent series resistance that is related to the bulk resistance of the electrode [54,55], wherein the smaller intercept can be found in the GQDs@MnO2 electrode, revealing the low bulk resistance of GQDs@MnO2 electrode with enhanced conductivity after the GQDs modification. Apart from this, the EIS plot of GQDs@MnO2 electrode demonstrates a smaller semicircle diameter and a more vertical straight line in the low-frequency region, suggesting that less obstacles for IL ion/electron transport and more ideal capacitive behaviors of GQDs@MnO2 electrode [55,56]. A more comprehensive analysis of the EIS results can be observed in Fig. 4h, which shows the relationship between frequency and phase angle of the pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes. By comparison, the GQDs@MnO2 electrode shows higher knee frequency and phase angle close to 90°, further confirming its favorable electrochemical kinetics in IL electrolyte. Fig. 4i provides a radar chart related to the overall electrochemical properties of pristine MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes in IL electrolyte, that is, cycle retention, rate capability, phase angle, mass and area specific capacities (Fig. 4i). Based on these results, it is clear that the GQDs@MnO2 electrode possesses faster kinetics, boosted capacitive storage, as well as superior rate and cycling capabilities than pristine MnO2 electrode in IL electrolyte. As we known, the common strategy is to combine MnO2 with various conductive carbon materials such as graphene and carbon nanotube. However, the introduction of graphene and carbon nanotube in the electrode could reduce the whole capacitance. By contrast, the coupling of GQDs on the surface of MnO2 can not only provide more electrochemical active sites to accumulate charges and facilitate the ion accessibility, but also promote the electrochemical reaction at the interface of electrolyte/electrode, thus greatly improving the electrochemical behaviors of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode without sacrificing the whole capacitance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and application demonstration of flexible supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Further clarifying the practical applications, a flexible IL-based supercapacitor with the sandwich configuration was constructed based on the GQDs@MnO2 composite and commercial activated carbon as cathode and anode materials (Fig. 5 a), respectively, wherein two electrodes were separated by the cellulose separator soaked in EMIMBF4 IL electrolyte, followed by encapsulated in two pieces of flexible packaging. When fully charged, a flexible IL-based supercapacitor (Fig. 5b) can readily power a red LED (1.5 W, 2.0 V). Multiple CV measurements of the fabricated flexible device are displayed in Fig. 5c. Evidently, the device retains its characteristic CV curves within a wide voltage range (0 ∼ 3.5 V) under various sweeping rates, suggesting its remarkable capacitive storage performance combined with high operating voltage. Additionally, the GCD curves exhibit nearly linear symmetric shapes at different current densities (Fig. 5d), revealing the good capacitive characteristics of the flexible supercapacitor. Ragone plot of the fabricated device based on the whole mass of the active materials is depicted in Fig. 5e. Our device is capable of generating high levels of energy density within a wide power range, specifically delivering a maximum energy density of 82.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1.75 kW kg−1, which is far superior to various electrochemical supercapacitors, PbO2/Pb and Ni/MH batteries, and even equivalent to Li-ion batteries. Furthermore, the maximum power density of our fabricated device can reach up to 11.6 kW kg−1 at an energy density of 42.5 Wh kg−1, which is comparable to that of conventional capacitors and significantly overperforms those of other energy storage devices [57,58]. More analysis about the electrochemical testing results of flexible IL-based supercapacitor is provided in Fig. S14.

In order to investigate the potential application of flexible IL-based supercapacitor in the field of portable electronics, the fabricated device was further subjected to electrochemical measurements upon the straight and bent states. During the deformation, the flexible supercapacitor can normally supply power for the electronic products (Fig. 5f). When the device was bent to around 90° angle, the specific capacitance can be well preserved with a low average decay rate of ∼ 0.37% per cycle during 2500 charging-discharging cycles. After restoring the device's shape to its original state, the specific capacitance remains steady during the following continuous 2500 cycles. In order to reduce the gas release in the soft-package IL-based supercapacitor, EMIMBF4 IL was vacuum-dried at 120 °C overnight before use. Vacuum-drying removes residue water from the IL electrolyte, so it stands to reason that the removal of water reduces the amount of gas produced. Although the reduction of some oxygen groups may lead to the gas release, the soft-package IL-based supercapacitor still exhibits good cycling performance after repeated cycles, while no bulging phenomenon happens in the soft-package device. A further important point is that the coulombic efficiency remains ∼ 100% during the entire cycling process, testifying the highly reversible charging/discharging process in the fabricated device throughout the repeated cycles. Therefore, the flexible IL-based supercapacitor is extremely appropriate for a broad range of applications in portable electronics and large-scale energy systems.

To summarize, we have designed a 3D nanoflower-like GQDs@MnO2 composite by synergistically coupling GQDs with MnO2 nanosheets. Combined the comprehensive electrochemical tests with DFT calculations, we found that the GQDs play a crucial role in modifying the density of state and electronic bandgap of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. As an electrode, the resultant GQDs@MnO2 composite possesses boosted capacitive storage, rapid charge–discharge response, and high electrochemical reversibility in IL electrolyte. Furtherly, a high-performance flexible IL-based supercapacitor has been successfully constructed using such GQDs@MnO2 composite electrode, which can deliver a high energy density (82.2 Wh kg−1), a high power density (11.6 kW kg−1), as well as good cycling performance under the straight and bent states. This work not only provides valuable reference for the research works related to the GQDs-based composites, but also offers a promising strategy for constructing high-performance IL-based supercapacitors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002157 and 61904116), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190976 and BK20211029), and the Science and Technology Project of State Grid Corporation of China (No. 5455DW190009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135301.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
